column2: Crafting a story in my head of what this picture meant to them. Maybe it was a girlfriend who's kidnapped or has gone missing and the doorbell ring is like the police coming to interview him. Another could be, it could have been his mother and his mother could have passed recently, and then that could be the doorbell could have been other family coming to tell him about the funeral.
column3: Theme 6: Building the Backstory

column2: It definitely helped [haptics]. Especially with the photo — I was flipping it around in my hands. And the wind was very unexpected. I think that was really nice as the fan panned towards me.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: I think it was actually to represent the wind blowing out the window, because I think I felt the gust at the same time when the photo flew out the window. So I think it wasn't the fan — I think it was actually to represent the wind.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: The photo was a really important aspect of the scene, and having the tactile experience with the photo helped me relate more to the character.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: I thought maybe it's on the ground so I could try and pick it up. But I think, as the participant recognising that I'm not actually this character — like maybe I'm representing some sort of entity that's viewing the scene, but not necessarily implicit within the scene. So I could still have some sort of impartiality to it.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: ── Participant N44J ──
He packed up, like, a soccer ball and then a teddy bear and then hesitated on like some pearls.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column3: Theme 6: Building the Backstory

column2: It was really, like, sad when it got blown away. Presumably it's like something sort of lost. And then he was really like, upset about the picture blowing away. So I was trying to give him back the picture for a while.
column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column2: I was really just drawn to the character and like what he was doing. And I felt like I was just kind of like, like observing for a long time and just kind of like watching it. That was like the biggest draw.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: I guess I felt like, like sympathy for the character, like a little bit. When I thought that like the picture was like gone, I was like, oh that's like sad, you know, like poor guy.
column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column2: I immediately like wanted to pick it up when I saw it, and then I initially wanted to like hand it back. And then afterwards I was just kind of looking at it.
column3: Theme 2: Frustration at Being Unable to Interact

column2: I noticed that it had the honey and I think it had like a date. It was like two thousand something — it was definitely like a past date. And then it was like him, and I think it was the character, I'm pretty sure was the character. And then some girl.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: I just wanted, like, initially I wanted to like throw it. I thought I would if I put it in front of the fan or something, it could like blow back. But then I was just like holding it. And I noticed if I let it go, it kind of stayed in the air. So then I was just kind of showing it.
column3: Theme 2: Frustration at Being Unable to Interact

column2: ── Participant T45J ──

There were some things that I was convinced were in the thing, like the fan, and then like the noise of the fan. And then it was kind of a surprise to feel that air on my hands, especially after the physical objects.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: It seemed just like a city-ish apartment with a single maybe guy in there, who had a picture of a current or former partner question mark, as well as some other items. A teddy bear was the first thing. A few items that they were picking up and putting down.
column3: Theme 6: Building the Backstory

column2: I was kind of either consciously or subconsciously holding the item in a similar way to how the character is holding it. Like I was given the necklace on my hands but the character was holding it like this, and so I kind of started holding it like that too.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: The necklace and the photo would have been like the same type of thing. And if I was in that character's shoes, I would have felt that thing. But somehow it felt different in vibe — like their experience would be warmer than my experience. Warmer emotionally. Like, I'm just some dude who came from my lab, and this guy probably has some backstory that they're dealing with, and I'm just holding a picture and they're holding the same picture.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: Everything was shared sensation-wise between me and this person, including the fan, until I could pick up the photo. And I was like, obviously there's no photo there. So I was just feeling my fingers. But that was something that was like completely my experience as a viewer versus this character's.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: The label on the picture — is that honey, I guess. I imagine if it hadn't said that, I would've just been like, oh, it's a picture, I don't know. Or at least I would have been less connected to the character.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: The haptics — it complicated things, not in a bad connotation, complicated. It just made more complex of — like you kind of become, or you're kind of put into the shoes of the character, and then all of a sudden, at least if you find the photo, then you're very, very much separated all of a sudden.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: When I pick up the picture of my own accord, I'm like, oh, I'm in a VR experience again. Versus like, I'm in this apartment.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: I was like, maybe there's something that happens if I'm able to give it back. I think I tried throwing it and it didn't convey. At some point I kind of stepped out of the role of in that apartment and then I was just kind of seeing what was possible with it — in like an engine sense, like, can I put it next to the fan and will it blow? Can I expand it if you pinch on two sides?
column3: Theme 2: Frustration at Being Unable to Interact

column2: At some point it transitioned from, like, here's some kind of thing that interacts with the character versus here's something that interacts with me as the participant in this experience.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

I saw who I think was a little boy. He seemed to be packing some sentimental items, and then he lost his photo. And I was sad for him. And then I found the photo, and I tried to give it back to him, but he didn't notice.
column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column2: I wanted to be helpful. Like, I was like, here, take it back.
column3: Theme 2: Frustration at Being Unable to Interact

There's a photo and like jewelry and a teddy bear. So it seemed like it was kind of charged. The sentimentality of the objects he was using.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: When he lost the photo, he looked very upset.
column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column3: Theme 5: Describing the Aesthetics of the Scene

column2: I noticed that the picture had him and I think it was a girl in it, which kind of added more to the — oh, you know, maybe this girl is gone or something.

I saw a living room. I saw a person coming in with a couple items and a box. Looked like some, like, apartment. And the person looked like he was moving.
column3: Theme 6: Building the Backstory

column2: The character's movement definitely did [stand out]. The movement was very, like, slow and very, like, lagging behind. So, like, it seemed like he was moving sluggishly.
column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column2: There was one where it felt like a ball. Another one where it picks up like a teddy bear, I felt that one. And a necklace — it was a beaded necklace. And there was another one that I felt, which was the fan. And like a photo-ish thing.
column3: Theme 3: Specificity in the Main Items of the Story

column2: It was very similar to what the character was picking up. When he was moving different objects into the box and he picked up a ball, then all of a sudden there was a ball in my hands. A sense that we're touching the same thing, we're feeling the same sensations.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: When it was picking up a teddy bear, it was like a fluffy object in my hand, and to the point where I was, like, matching the same orientation that he was when he picked things up to sort of like, match how he was feeling.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: Like a bit — not like pity, but like sympathy. Felt very sympathetic towards what he was going through. I didn't know exactly what he was going through, but I knew that it didn't encourage, like, a loss of some kind. Because it was like a photograph with him and this girl, and she wasn't in the scene at all. So I assumed that something happened.
column3: Theme 6: Building the Backstory

column2: The movements felt very realistic, so I feel like that helped a lot. The entire world was very stylish and cartoony, so I kind of expected something a little bit more animated in movement. But his movements mimicked like a human in the sense that every single movement wasn't exaggerated. It was kind of natural looking. Even to the point where he was picking up the objects, he didn't look like he was grabbing it — it looked like he was like, directly being moved by it. The animation style really helped sell that kind of feeling to make me sympathize with him.
column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels

column2: Maybe hearing him — some kind of sound come out of him. I couldn't really see any facial expressions, so I couldn't really tell what exactly he was feeling. I can just tell from his body movements and language. Either him making a sound or more animated face would have definitely helped.
column3: Theme 5: Describing the Aesthetics of the Scene

column2: It definitely helped [haptics]. It made it feel a lot more immersive because you can really see from a screen what someone is going through, but to like, feel what they're feeling at the same time definitely helps you put yourself in their shoes and really imagine what it's like from their perspective.
column3: Theme 1: Negotiating Connection With the Character

column2: I kind of felt helpless in that moment because I couldn't pick it up. And then I was just like, watching him frantically look around, search around for it still, and I just felt like I couldn't really help him feel a little bit better.
column3: Theme 2: Frustration at Being Unable to Interact

column3: Theme 4: Describing the Way the Character Feels
